exemplar.title: How does increasing temperature (25, 35, 45, 55, and 65Â°C) affect the permeability of cell membranes in Beta vulgaris L., as measured by the amount of pigment leakage quantified through colorimeter absorbance values?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does increasing temperature (25, 35, 45, 55, and 65Â°C) affect the permeability of cell membranes in Beta vulgaris L., as measured by the amount of pigment leakage quantified through colorimeter absorbance values?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is described within a specific system (Beta vulgaris vacuolar pigment leakage), giving strong context.
Methodological considerations (temperature intervals, exposure time, controlled variables) are clearly described and linked to data relevance.
Procedure steps include dimensions, volumes and timings, allowing reproduction with few ambiguities.
weaknesses: No real-world application (e.g., impact on post-harvest storage) is mentioned, limiting broader contextual relevance.
Inconsistent terminology in hypothesis and data tables (âabsorbanceâ vs âtransmissionâ) introduces conceptual confusion.
Instrument details (colorimeter make/model, calibration procedure) and cooling rate standardisation are omitted, affecting full reproducibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (methodological considerations and methodology description) clearly fall in the 3â4 band, and although the research question context meets the 5â6 band, minor contextual gaps prevent a full top score. An overall score of 4 reflects solid design with room for deeper real-world linkage and improved consistency.

strengths: Data processing (means, standard deviations, one-way ANOVA, eta-squared) is carried out appropriately and interpreted correctly.
Processed results are presented in clear tables and graphs with error bars, facilitating understanding of trends and statistical decisions.
Uncertainty of the colorimeter is quoted, showing some awareness of measurement limitations.
weaknesses: Consideration of uncertainties is limited: no propagation of the Â±0.5â%T or sampling errors into statistical analysis.
Inconsistent labeling and missing units (mixing absorbance/transmission, unlabeled axes) reduce precision.
Notation errors (mean formula miswritten), omitted temperature row in SD table, and lack of post-hoc analysis weaken completeness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Processing of data is accurate and appropriate (5â6 band), but communication has inconsistencies and uncertainties are only superficially addressed (placing it in the 3â4 band). A score of 4 rewards strong analysis while noting omissions in labeling and uncertainty treatment.

strengths: Conclusion is justified, relevant to the research question and fully consistent with the statistical analysis (trend and p-value referenced).
Comparison to literature on protein denaturation thresholds demonstrates awareness of scientific context.
weaknesses: Literature comparison is brief and lacks deeper discussion of membrane phase transitions or species variability.
Conclusion would benefit from a concise summary tying quantitative effect size (Î·Â²) to biological implications.
Scientific context could be expanded with more detailed references to accepted membrane-leakage models.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is fully consistent with the analysis (band 5â6) but the scientific context comparison is only moderately developed (band 3â4). A score of 3 reflects a justified conclusion with room for richer contextualization.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (limited replicates, single-plant sampling, size variation, light interference) are identified and described.
Realistic improvements (increase sample size, randomize across plants, standardize cutting and cooling, shield colorimeter) are proposed and linked to weaknesses.
weaknesses: Relative impact of each identified limitation is not evaluated or ranked, limiting depth of the evaluation.
Improvements lack explanation of how they would quantitatively enhance precision or validity.
Strengths table entry (âRange of temperatures testedâ) is incomplete, reducing clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Weaknesses and improvements are specific and relevant (band 3â4), but the lack of explanation of their relative impact and quantitative enhancement places this criterion in the 1â2 band. A score of 2 recognizes the concrete suggestions while noting the need for deeper evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The conclusion restates that increasing temperature increases membrane permeability and ties this directly to the decreasing transmission values and to the statistically significant ANOVA result.  The justification references both the trend and the p-value, ensuring full consistency with the analysis.  Slightly stronger quantitative reference (e.g., citing exact Î·Â² magnitude) would perfect the argument, so a score of 5 in the top band is appropriate.

Raw data are laid out in clearly labelled tables with units and significant figures; processed means, SDs and ANOVA outputs are tabulated and the graph includes error bars.  Presentation is **clear** and for the most part precise, though headings occasionally mix âabsorbanceâ and âtransmissionâ and axis units are not shown on the miniature image.  Minor inconsistencies prevent awarding the fully precise top band.

Means, standard deviations, a one-way ANOVA and eta-squared effect size are all correctly executed and interpreted, directly addressing the research question.  Calculations appear accurate and the statistical decisions (Î± = 0.05, rejection of Hâ) are justified.  Minor formula mis-typesetting does not affect correctness, so the processing is **appropriate and accurate**, warranting the 5-6 band; a single small computational slip would keep it at 5 rather than 6.

Specific methodological weaknesses are **listed and described**: limited trials, single-plant sampling, hand-cut size variation, external light interference.  Each weakness is explained in terms of its possible effect (reliability, generalisability, measurement error).  The relative *impact* (which factor is most serious) is not analysed in depth, so description rather than full explanation is achieved, aligning with the 3-4 band, score 3.

The student compares findings to literature on protein denaturation thresholds (Niu & Xiang 2018; Zhou & Labuza 2011) and notes consistency with known temperature limits.  This comparison is **relevant but brief**, lacking deeper discussion of membrane phase transitions or variability among species.  Consequently the comparison is present and pertinent yet not fully elaborated, fitting the 3-4 band at a mid-level score of 3.

The research question is clearly stated and rooted in a **specific biological system (Beta vulgaris vacuolar pigment leakage)**.  The introduction supplies concise yet targeted background on membrane structure, beetroot betalain pigments and colourimetry, allowing the reader to understand *why* temperature should influence permeability and *how* this can be measured.  The context moves beyond a generic statement to link temperatureâinduced protein denaturation directly to the anticipated absorbance change, demonstrating an appropriate and specific framing.

Minor contextual gaps remain (e.g. no explicit mention of real-world relevance such as post-harvest storage of crops), preventing the fullest possible sophistication, but the coverage still matches the descriptor of a âspecific and appropriate context.â

Uncertainty of the colorimeter (Â±0.5 %T) is quoted, but **no propagation** or incorporation into calculated means, graphs or statistics is attempted, and sampling uncertainty (mass, temperature fluctuation) is ignored.  Evidence of consideration is therefore *limited* and contains substantial omissions, matching the 1-2 band.

For every identified weakness a **realistic improvement** is proposed (increase replicates, use different beetroots, stricter cutting checks, shield colorimeter from light).  The improvements are linked to the weakness but the student does not fully *explain* how each change would quantitatively enhance precision or validity.  Thus the response sits comfortably in the âdescribedâ level of the 3-4 band.

The student **identifies key methodological considerations**: rationale for the chosen temperature interval, justification of 10 min exposure, control of surface area, water volume, light wavelength and use of distilled water.  These considerations are *described* and linked to how they help produce relevant data.

However, the adequacy of the data set (only five temperatures and three replicates) is not critically considered, nor is pigment concentration calibration or sample randomisation; hence the explanation of sufficiency is partial.  Description is solid but not fully *explained* in the depth expected for the top band.

Steps are numbered and give dimensions, times, volumes and instrument settings so that another investigator could reasonably reproduce the procedure.  Ambiguities remain (exact model/setting of colourimeter, calibration method, rate of cooling before measurement), meaning some clarification would be required on replication.  Therefore the description is reproducible *with few omissions*, aligning with the 3-4 band rather than the completely omission-free top band.

annotations.comment: Figure 1 lacks clear axis titles and units on both axes. Ensure the bar chart displays labelled axes, error bars, and a legend where appropriate.
The ANOVA interpretation states that a small $$F$$ means little difference. Instead, relate $$F$$ to the ratio of betweenÂ­group to withinÂ­group variance for accurate interpretation.
The ANOVA results appropriately justify rejecting $$H_0$$ at $$p=0.0171<0.05$$. Good clear linkage of test to decision rule.
Consider postÂ­hoc analysis (e.g., Tukeyâs HSD) to identify which temperature groups differ, strengthening the statistical evaluation beyond the omnibus ANOVA.
The eta-squared value ($$n^2=0.531$$) suggests a strong effect, but referencing typical threshold values or literature benchmarks would contextualise its magnitude.
The conclusion restates findings but lacks a concise summary statement tying the key statistical result and biological implication together.
Comparison to literature is relevant but brief. Expand discussion on membrane phase transitions and speciesÂ­specific responses to strengthen scientific context.
The strengths table is missing the full explanation for âRange of temperatures tested.â Complete this entry to convey the strengthâs impact clearly.
Improvement suggestions link to weaknesses but lack explanation of how each change quantitatively enhances reliability or validity. Elaborate on the expected impact.

The research question is well framed within a specific system (Beta vulgaris vacuolar pigment leakage), showing strong context. Consider adding a brief realâworld application (e.g., postâharvest crop storage) to deepen relevance.

The introduction to membrane structure is overly general and lengthy. Focus on the aspects directly tied to pigment leakage and colorimetry to streamline background and strengthen linkage to the research question.
The hypothesis incorrectly refers to âlight absorption transmission,â mixing terms. Clarify whether you are measuring absorbance or transmission to avoid conceptual confusion.
The colorimeter description omits make and model, impeding reproducibility. Specify the exact instrument and calibration procedure so others can replicate measurements.
The rationale for the 10 Â°C temperature increments is described but lacks quantitative justification. Include preliminary data tables or statistics to support interval selection.
The dependent variable description mentions absorbance but raw data table lists transmission (%T). Ensure consistency in terminology and units throughout for clarity.
The controlled variables table is comprehensive but could note the cooling rate standardisation after heating to prevent variable cooling times affecting pigment leakage.
Using samples all from one beet limits generalisability. Acknowledge this and consider randomising across multiple beets to capture natural variability in betalin content.
The materials list still reads âinsert type used hereâ for the colorimeter. Specify exact model and settings to ensure the methodology is fully reproducible.
Step 8 instructs cooling to room temperature without timing. This introduces variability; standardise a fixed cooling duration or measure sample temperature before cuvette transfer.
The raw data table mixes absorbance and transmission. Units are missing for axes. Add clear labels and units in the table caption and any graphs.
Only the colorimeterâs Â±0.5 %T uncertainty is quoted but never propagated into SD or ANOVA. Include these uncertainties in calculations or discuss their impact.
The mean formula is miswritten. It should read $$\text{mean}=\frac{\sum \text{data}}{N}$$. Correct the notation for clarity.
The standard deviation table omits the 65 Â°C row. Include all temperature levels in the SD table to match the mean data set.
The discussion of high SD claims it âdoes not affect results greatlyâ without quantitative support. Reflect on how variability influences confidence in trends and statistical power.

The research question is stated without context.

The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data lacks the detail to allow for the investigation to be reproduced.
The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data allows for the investigation to be reproduced with few ambiguities or omissions.
The research question is outlined within a broad context.
Methodological considerations associated with collecting relevant and sufficient data to answer the research question are described.
The research question is described within a specific and appropriate context.
Methodological considerations associated with collecting relevant and sufficient data to answer the research question are explained.
The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data allows for the investigation to be reproduced.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
The recording and processing of the data is communicated but is neither clear nor precise.
Some processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out but with major omissions, inaccuracies or inconsistencies.
The recording and processing of data shows limited evidence of the consideration of uncertainties.
The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out but with some significant omissions, inaccuracies or inconsistencies.
The communication of the recording and processing of the data is either clear or precise.
The recording and processing of data shows evidence of a consideration of uncertainties but with some significant omissions or inaccuracies.
The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out appropriately and accurately.
The recording and processing of data shows evidence of an appropriate consideration of uncertainties.
The communication of the recording and processing of the data is both clear and precise.

rubricCriterionStrands.name: Communication of data recording and processing

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content: No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
The conclusion makes superficial comparison to the accepted scientific context.
A conclusion is stated that is relevant to the research question but is not supported by the analysis presented.
A conclusion is described that makes some relevant comparison to the accepted scientific context.
A conclusion is described that is relevant to the research question but is not fully consistent with the analysis presented.
A conclusion is justified that is relevant to the research question and fully consistent with the analysis presented.
A conclusion is justified through relevant comparison to the accepted scientific context.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
Realistic improvements to the investigation are stated.
The report states generic methodological weaknesses or limitations.
Realistic improvements to the investigation that are relevant to the identified weaknesses or limitations, are described.
The report describes specific methodological weaknesses or limitations.
Realistic improvements to the investigation, that are relevant to the identified weaknesses or limitations, are explained.
The report explains the relative impact of specific methodological weaknesses or limitations.
